---
title: "11 · Polynomials — Adding and Multiplying"
subtitle: "Sums of powers of a variable — and the rectangle picture, generalised"
lesson-id: "t2.polynomials"
tier: 2
prerequisites: ["t1.intro-variables", "t1.multiply-as-area", "t2.exponents-powers"]
unlocks: ["t2.factoring-polynomials", "t2.quadratic-equations-intro"]
applied-domains: ["research", "encryption"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T1 L11 — Variables](../../tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)
   and [07 · Exponents](../07-exponents-powers/index.qmd)?
2. Can you compute $5x^2$ for $x = 3$ and get 45?
3. Can you multiply two single-digit numbers in your head?
:::

## Touch

Pick two binomials. The widget multiplies them and shows the *rectangle*
picture (Tier 1 L05 generalised).

```{ojs}
viewof aT = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 1, step: 1, label: "a (in (ax + b)):"})
viewof bT = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 3, step: 1, label: "b:"})
viewof cT = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 1, step: 1, label: "c (in (cx + d)):"})
viewof dT = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "d:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  // (ax + b)(cx + d) = ac x^2 + (ad + bc) x + bd
  const x2 = aT * cT, x1 = aT * dT + bT * cT, x0 = bT * dT;
  const fmt = (n, sym) => {
    if (n === 0) return "";
    const sign = n > 0 ? "+ " : "− ";
    const mag = Math.abs(n);
    const coeff = mag === 1 && sym ? "" : mag;
    return ` ${sign}${coeff}${sym}`;
  };
  const result = (x2 === 0 ? "" : `${x2}x²`) + fmt(x1, "x") + fmt(x0, "");
  return html`<div style="text-align:center; margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1.4rem;">
      (${aT}x ${bT >= 0 ? '+' : '−'} ${Math.abs(bT)})(${cT}x ${dT >= 0 ? '+' : '−'} ${Math.abs(dT)})
      &nbsp;=&nbsp;
      <span style="color:#c2185b; font-weight:bold;">${result.trim().replace(/^\+ /, '')}</span>
    </div>
    <div style="font-size:0.95rem; color:#666; margin-top:0.5rem; font-family:monospace;">
      x² coeff: ${aT}·${cT} = ${x2} &nbsp; · &nbsp; x coeff: ${aT}·${dT} + ${bT}·${cT} = ${x1} &nbsp; · &nbsp; constant: ${bT}·${dT} = ${x0}
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

A **polynomial** is a sum of *terms*, each of which is a number times
a power of $x$. Multiplying two polynomials is exactly the *area of a
rectangle* with side-lengths given by each polynomial — split into
sub-rectangles. The Tier 1 picture survives.

## Wonder

1. What kind of expression is $3x^2 + 5x - 7$? How many terms?
2. Is $3x + 2 + 5x$ the same as $8x + 2$? Why?
3. What does $(x + 1)(x + 1)$ give?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Add: $(2x^2 + 3x - 5) + (x^2 - 2x + 8)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Combine **like terms**: $x^2$ with $x^2$, $x$ with $x$, constants
with constants.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2$: $2 + 1 = 3$. $x$: $3 - 2 = 1$. Constant: $-5 + 8 = 3$.
**$3x^2 + x + 3$.**

Adding polynomials is just adding the *coefficients* of matching
powers — like sorting items by category before counting.
</details>

### Problem 2

Multiply: $3x \cdot (2x + 5)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Distribute the $3x$ across both terms inside the bracket.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3x \cdot 2x + 3x \cdot 5 = 6x^2 + 15x$.

Recall the exponent rule: $x \cdot x = x^2$. Multiplying a number by
the same variable adds 1 to the variable's exponent.
</details>

### Problem 3

Multiply: $(x + 2)(x + 3)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Picture a rectangle with sides $(x + 2)$ and $(x + 3)$. Split into 4
sub-rectangles.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The four sub-rectangles have areas $x^2, 3x, 2x, 6$. Sum: $x^2 + 5x +
6$.

Equivalently, distribute: $(x + 2)(x + 3) = x(x + 3) + 2(x + 3) = x^2
+ 3x + 2x + 6 = x^2 + 5x + 6$.

The widget shows the same result: $x^2$ coefficient = 1, $x$
coefficient = $1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 1 = 5$, constant = $2 \cdot 3 = 6$.

This pattern generalises into the *FOIL* mnemonic (First, Outer,
Inner, Last) — but the rectangle picture is more honest and
generalises beyond binomials.
</details>

### Problem 4

Multiply: $(x + 5)(x - 5)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the rectangle picture or distribute carefully.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 - 5x + 5x - 25 = x^2 - 25$. The middle terms **cancel**.

This is the **difference of squares** identity:
$$(x + a)(x - a) = x^2 - a^2.$$
A pattern worth memorising — it appears constantly when factoring
(Lesson 12) and in higher algebra.
</details>

### Problem 5

Multiply: $(x + 3)^2$ — that is, $(x + 3)(x + 3)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 + 3x + 3x + 9 = x^2 + 6x + 9$.

This is the **square of a binomial** identity:
$$(x + a)^2 = x^2 + 2ax + a^2.$$

**Common error alert:** $(x + a)^2 \neq x^2 + a^2$. The $2ax$ in the
middle is *essential*. We met this gotcha briefly in Tier 2 L07; it
returns to bite anyone who forgets it.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The first systematic study of polynomial-like expressions appears in
the ***Arithmetica*** of **Diophantus of Alexandria** (c. 250 CE) — a
13-book treasure-house of algebraic problems. Diophantus had no
modern symbol for the unknown; he used Greek abbreviations, written in
prose. It was his book, scribbled in the margin a thousand years
later, that prompted **Pierre de Fermat** in 1637 to claim he had a
"truly marvellous proof" of what we now call **Fermat's Last
Theorem** — a proof finally completed by Andrew Wiles in 1994.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A polynomial is just a tidy list of *how much of each power of $x$* you
have. Adding two polynomials sorts the powers and adds in columns;
multiplying them is the rectangle picture, with one side per polynomial.
Every algebraic identity you will ever meet is some pattern hiding in
those columns.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **polynomial in one variable** is a sum of **terms**, each of the
form $c \cdot x^k$ where $c$ is a constant and $k$ is a non-negative
integer.

The **degree** of a polynomial is the *highest* exponent of $x$
present.

| Degree | Name |
|:-:|---|
| 0 | constant ($5$) |
| 1 | linear ($3x + 2$) |
| 2 | quadratic ($x^2 - 4x + 7$) |
| 3 | cubic ($x^3 + 2x - 1$) |
| 4 | quartic |
| 5 | quintic |

**Adding polynomials:** combine like terms (same power of $x$).

**Multiplying polynomials:** distribute every term in the first across
every term in the second; then collect like terms.

**Two key identities:**

- **Difference of squares:** $(x + a)(x - a) = x^2 - a^2$.
- **Square of a binomial:** $(x + a)^2 = x^2 + 2ax + a^2$.

:::

> *Same powers add as like terms; different powers stand alone;*
> *To multiply: every term in one across every term in the other.*
> *Difference of squares cancels the middle; square of a binomial doubles it.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Multiply $(2x - 3)(x + 4)$.

A competent solver distributes systematically:

1. $2x \cdot x = 2x^2$.
2. $2x \cdot 4 = 8x$.
3. $-3 \cdot x = -3x$.
4. $-3 \cdot 4 = -12$.
5. Combine: $2x^2 + (8 - 3)x - 12 = 2x^2 + 5x - 12$.

**Answer: $2x^2 + 5x - 12$.** Check on the widget: $a = 2, b = -3, c =
1, d = 4$ → $2x^2 + 5x - 12$ ✓.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Add $(3x + 5) + (4x - 2)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$7x + 3$.
</details>

**W2.** Multiply $4x \cdot (x + 7)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4x^2 + 28x$.
</details>

**W3.** Multiply $(x + 4)(x - 4)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Difference of squares.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 - 16$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Multiply $(x + 2)^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 + 4x + 4$.
</details>

**P2.** Multiply $(2x + 1)(3x - 5)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$6x^2 - 10x + 3x - 5 = 6x^2 - 7x - 5$.
</details>

**P3.** Subtract $(3x^2 + 4x - 1) - (x^2 - 2x + 5)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Distribute the negative sign across the second polynomial.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(3x^2 - x^2) + (4x - (-2x)) + (-1 - 5) = 2x^2 + 6x - 6$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Multiply $(x + 1)(x^2 - x + 1)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Distribute carefully — three terms in the second polynomial.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x \cdot (x^2 - x + 1) + 1 \cdot (x^2 - x + 1) = x^3 - x^2 + x + x^2 -
x + 1 = x^3 + 1$.

This is another famous identity: **sum of cubes** — $x^3 + 1 = (x +
1)(x^2 - x + 1)$. (And generally $x^3 + a^3 = (x + a)(x^2 - ax + a^2)$.)

Polynomials have many such identities — they are the alphabet of
algebraic manipulation. Memorising the most common ones (difference of
squares, square of a binomial, sum/difference of cubes) saves time.
</details>

**S2.** **Polynomials are everywhere in encryption.** RSA encryption
treats messages as numbers and *polynomials over modular arithmetic*
appear in advanced schemes. **Lattice-based post-quantum
cryptography** literally lives in *polynomial rings*: $\mathbb{Z}[x] /
(x^n + 1)$ — polynomials modulo another polynomial. Why might
polynomials be useful for cryptography?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Polynomials provide structured complexity.** They are large enough
(many coefficients) to carry significant information; they have rich
algebraic structure (addition, multiplication, identities); and
operations on them are well-studied and efficient.

Polynomial rings allow constructions like **error-correcting codes**
(Reed-Solomon — used in CDs, DVDs, satellite communication) and
**lattice cryptography** (NTRU, Kyber — currently being standardised
as post-quantum cryptography by NIST).

The humble polynomial of Tier 2 is the entry point to topics that
secure modern digital infrastructure. Tier 6 (abstract algebra) builds
the theory; Tier 7 (encryption track) applies it.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$(x + a)^2 = x^2 + a^2$."** **False.** The correct expansion is
   $x^2 + 2ax + a^2$. The cross-term is essential.
2. **"Like terms are terms with the same number coefficient."** No —
   like terms have the *same variable raised to the same power*. $3x$
   and $7x$ are like terms; $3x$ and $7x^2$ are not.
3. **"Multiplying always increases the degree."** Yes — multiplying a
   degree-$m$ polynomial by a degree-$n$ polynomial gives degree
   $m + n$. *Adding* two polynomials of the same degree, however, can
   sometimes *reduce* the degree (e.g., $x^2 + (-x^2 + 3) = 3$, a
   constant).
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [polynomial](/glossary.qmd#polynomial) is a sum of powers of $x$,
   each scaled by a number.** Adding combines like powers; multiplying
   distributes every term across every term.
2. **The [degree](/glossary.qmd#degree-of-a-polynomial) of a polynomial
   is its highest power of $x$.** Multiplying adds degrees;
   that one fact predicts the shape of the answer before you compute.
3. **Two patterns are worth burning into memory:** the
   [difference of squares](/glossary.qmd#difference-of-squares)
   $(x+a)(x-a) = x^2 - a^2$ and the square of a binomial
   $(x+a)^2 = x^2 + 2ax + a^2$.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you derive the difference-of-squares identity $(x + a)(x - a)
   = x^2 - a^2$ in your own words?
2. Without scrolling back, can you multiply $(x + 3)(x - 7)$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $x^2 - 4x - 21$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[12 · Factoring Polynomials](../12-factoring-polynomials/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (sum of cubes) — it appears in many later
  contexts.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3, P4, P5* — the three identities
  are the bedrock.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T1 L11 — Variables](../../tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)**
- **[T1 L05 — Multiplication as Area](../../tier-1-foundations/05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)**
- **[07 · Exponents and Powers](../07-exponents-powers/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **[12 · Factoring Polynomials](../12-factoring-polynomials/index.qmd)** —
  the *inverse* of multiplication.
- **[14 · Quadratic Equations](../14-quadratic-equations-intro/index.qmd)** —
  solving $x^2 + bx + c = 0$.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** Polynomial rings underpin lattice-based post-quantum
cryptography (NIST has standardised CRYSTALS-Kyber, an NTRU-style
scheme). Polynomial codes (Reed-Solomon) protect every CD, DVD,
QR code, and deep-space satellite signal.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Polynomial rings are *the* central object
of commutative algebra and algebraic geometry — entire branches of
modern mathematics. The Hilbert Nullstellensatz, the Gröbner basis
algorithm, the Riemann hypothesis (in its function-field analogue) —
all live in polynomial-ring language.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Engineering & physics.** Polynomial *fitting* is the simplest curve-
fit method (extending the linear regression of Lesson 5). Splines —
piecewise polynomials — model smooth curves in CAD, fonts, and
animation.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Polynomial models appear in finance (yield
curves), economics (marginal cost = derivative of a polynomial),
statistics (regression with quadratic and higher terms), and
computer graphics (Bezier and B-spline curves are polynomial in
nature).
:::
